spec_title: BRC-31 mutual-auth conformance vectors (General-message signable payload + signature byte-lock)
description: Byte-locks the canonical BRC-104 General-message signable payload (build_request_payload output), the derived signing key_id, and the deterministic RFC-6979 ECDSA signature, all produced FROM the proven in-repo wire in bsv-mpc (crates/bsv-mpc-core/src/brc31_client.rs + tests/conformance_07_brc31_auth.rs). The signature is signed with key = client identity derived via BRC-42 protocol [2, "auth message signature"], key_id = "{request_nonce} {server_session_nonce}", counterparty = Other(server_identity), over SHA-256(signable_payload). Verification (verify_message_signature) derives the corresponding public key and checks the signature over SHA-256(signable_payload).
notes: ALL inputs are fixed/deterministic test-only values. sender_privkey = 0x11*32, server_privkey = 0x22*32, stranger_privkey = 0x33*32 (raw 32-byte scalars, NOT seed phrases).
request_id = 0x11*32; request_nonce = base64(0xC3*32); server_session_nonce = base64(0xA1*32). Real deployments MUST use fresh CSPRNG 32-byte nonces.

BRC-104 signable payload framing (bsv_rs::auth::transports::HttpRequest::to_payload, NO leading version byte): [request_id 32B][method: varint(len)+utf8][path: varint(len)+utf8, or varint(-1) if empty][search: varint(len)+utf8, or varint(-1) if empty][headers: varint(count) + (varint(klen)+key + varint(vlen)+val)*][body: varint(len)+bytes, or varint(-1) if empty].

varint(-1) (empty path/search/body sentinel) encodes as the 9 bytes 0xFF 0xFF*8 in bsv-rs 0.3.11 — see write_varint(). The empty search field in these vectors is therefore the 9-byte run ffffffffffffffffff in signable_payload_hex.

Signable headers are filtered to {content-type, authorization, x-bsv-* (excl x-bsv-auth-*)} and sorted alphabetically. These vectors carry exactly one signable header: content-type: application/json (media type only, params stripped).
For General messages, signing_data() == the signable payload, and key_id == "{nonce} {your_nonce}" == "{request_nonce} {server_session_nonce}".
ECDSA is RFC-6979 deterministic (low-s normalized, DER-encoded): the signature_hex is byte-locked and reproduces identically across runs (verified: two consecutive signings byte-equal).

BYTE-LOCKED: signable_payload_hex, key_id, signature_hex (DER). VERIFY-ONLY in the cross-validator: the signature is verified (not regenerated) against verify_pubkey_hex using plain ECDSA over SHA-256(signable_payload), because reproducing BRC-42-derived-key signing in a second implementation is out of scope for the byte-lock cross-validator.

verify_pubkey_hex is the BRC-42-derived public key the canonical server verifier reconstructs (counterparty = Other(sender_identity), protocol [2, "auth message signature"], key_id as above, for_self=false). It is emitted FROM the proven wire, not hand-computed.

CROSS-IMPL STATUS: this vector is authored + byte-locked + cross-validated within the Calhoun (bsv-mpc) implementation and the independent Python/Rust byte-lock paths. FINAL cross-impl lock requires the partner implementation (rust-mpc) to consume this vector and reproduce signable_payload_hex byte-for-byte and verify the signature against verify_pubkey_hex. Until then, treat the signature_hex as Calhoun-side-proven, not yet cross-impl-proven.

vectors.name: valid-general-message
vectors.description: Canonical client signs a General request; the canonical server verifier accepts it. Signable payload + key_id + signature are byte-locked.

vectors.name: wrong-identity-rejected
vectors.description: A signature produced by a different identity key (stranger_privkey) over the same payload, but the server session is bound to sender_identity. The canonical verifier MUST reject it.

expected.rejection_reason: signature was produced by stranger_privkey, but verify_pubkey is derived from the session-bound sender_identity — the curve point does not match, ECDSA verify fails

vectors.name: tampered-payload-rejected
vectors.description: The valid signature, but the signed BRC-104 payload's last byte is flipped after signing (0x7d -> 0x82). The canonical verifier MUST reject it (payload binding).

expected.rejection_reason: signature is over SHA-256(original_payload); the verifier hashes the tampered_payload, the digests differ, ECDSA verify fails
